content.text: combines a vector similarity search with a scalar predicate -- for example, "find the 10 most relevant documents about machine learning that were published after 2023-01-01 and tagged as peer-reviewed."  This is the dominant access pattern in production RAG systems, where you need semantic relevance

structured constraints simultaneously.  The three fundamental strategies -- post-filter, pre-filter, and filter-aware ANN -- each have a different performance/recall tradeoff, and choosing the wrong one for a given filter selectivity is one of the most common sources of poor RAG performance.

attrs.alt: Three metadata filtering strategies: post-filter (ANN first, apply filter to results -- fast but misses results for selective filters); pre-filter (evaluate filter first, build candidate bitset, ANN within that set -- correct for any selectivity but slow for high-cardinality filters); filter-aware ANN (check filter condition at each HNSW node during traversal -- best recall for selective filters).

content.text: is the fraction of vectors in the index that satisfy the filter predicate.  It is the most important factor in choosing a filtering strategy:
Very poor -- most ANN candidates filtered out
Filter-aware ANN or pre-filter with index
Filter-aware ANN or post-filter with 5x over-fetch

occurs when the filter is highly selective.  If only 0.1% of vectors match the filter and you request top-5 with ANN over 1M vectors: ANN returns its best 100 candidates, but the filter eliminates 99.9% of them, leaving 0 results.  This is not a bug -- it is a fundamental consequence of applying a filter after approximate nearest-neighbour search.

Post-filter is the simplest implementation: run ANN search to get the top-K approximate neighbours, then discard any results that fail the scalar predicate.  It is correct for high-selectivity filters but produces poor recall for selective filters unless K is made proportionally larger.

# Chroma: post-filter (default behaviour)
results = collection.query(
    query_texts=['transformer attention mechanisms'],
    n_results=5,
    where={'source': {'$eq': 'arxiv'}},  # post-filter applied after ANN
)

# For selective filters, over-fetch to compensate:
# If only 10% of docs are from arxiv, request 50 to get 5 after filtering
results = collection.query(
    query_texts=['transformer attention mechanisms'],
    n_results=50,            # over-fetch 10x
    where={'source': {'$eq': 'arxiv-papers'}},
)
# Use only the first 5 from filtered results
filtered = results['ids'][0][:5]

# Pinecone: post-filter on serverless
results = index.query(
    vector=embedding,
    top_k=10,
    filter={'source': {'$eq': 'arxiv'}},
    include_metadata=True,
)
The over-fetch multiplier needed to maintain recall:
For selectivity = 0.05 (5% of vectors match) and n = 5, fetch at least 100 candidates:
candidates -- at that point, post-filter is not viable.

Pre-filter evaluates the scalar predicate first, builds a bitset of matching IDs, and then runs ANN search constrained to that bitset.  This gives correct recall at any selectivity but requires a fast scalar index on the filter fields.

from pymilvus import MilvusClient

client = MilvusClient(uri='http://localhost:19530')
client.load_collection('articles')

# Milvus: scalar filter expression evaluated before ANN
# 'source == "arxiv"' uses the TRIE/BITMAP scalar index on the 'source' field
results = client.search(
    collection_name='articles',
    data=[embedding],
    limit=5,
    filter='source == "arxiv" and published_year >= 2023',
    search_params={'metric_type': 'COSINE', 'params': {'ef': 64}},
    output_fields=['title', 'source', 'published_year'],
)

# When the filter is very selective (< 1%), Milvus builds a candidate bitset
# and runs HNSW exclusively within that subset.
# For low-selectivity filters (> 50%), Milvus falls back to post-filter
# automatically (it's faster in that regime).

# Check search plan via explain (if enabled in Milvus config):
# 'PreFilteredSearch: true' confirms pre-filter mode was used
To make pre-filter fast, create a scalar index on each frequently-filtered field:

Filter-aware ANN checks the scalar predicate at each node during HNSW graph traversal.  Nodes that fail the filter are skipped but still used as navigation hops (their neighbours are explored).  This is the most effective strategy across all selectivity ranges: it has no over-fetch overhead for high-selectivity filters, and for low-selectivity filters it naturally explores more of the graph.
To maintain recall with selective filters, the search beam width (
in HNSW) must be increased.  The intuition: if only 1% of vectors match, you need to explore ~100x more graph nodes to find the same number of matching candidates.

import weaviate, weaviate.classes as wvc

client = weaviate.connect_to_local()
articles = client.collections.get('Article')

# Weaviate: filter-aware ANN via nearText + where
# The HNSW traversal checks the where filter at each candidate node
results = articles.query.near_text(
    query='transformer attention mechanisms',
    limit=5,
    filters=(
        wvc.query.Filter.by_property('source').equal('arxiv')
        & wvc.query.Filter.by_property('published_year').greater_than(2022)
    ),
    return_metadata=wvc.query.MetadataQuery(distance=True),
)

for obj in results.objects:
    print(obj.properties['title'], f'distance={obj.metadata.distance:.3f}')

# For selective filters, Weaviate automatically increases ef internally.
# You can also configure efSearch per collection:
# weaviate.yaml: vectorIndexConfig.ef: 128  (default: 64)

client.close()
Each database uses a different filter expression language.  The supported operators and composition rules vary:
== != > >= < <= in not in and or like
Rebuild index for subset, or use LangChain FAISS with metadata

# Comprehensive filter examples across databases

# ── Pinecone: compound filter ──
results = index.query(
    vector=embedding,
    top_k=5,
    filter={
        '$and': [
            {'source': {'$in': ['arxiv', 'nature', 'science']}},
            {'year': {'$gte': 2023}},
            {'peer_reviewed': {'$eq': True}},
        ]
    },
)

# ── Milvus: expression filter ──
results = client.search(
    'articles',
    data=[embedding],
    limit=5,
    filter='source in ["arxiv", "nature"] and year >= 2023 and peer_reviewed == True',
    search_params={'metric_type': 'COSINE', 'params': {'ef': 128}},
)

# ── Chroma: MongoDB-style filter ──
results = collection.query(
    query_texts=['transformer attention'],
    n_results=5,
    where={
        '$and': [
            {'source': {'$in': ['arxiv', 'nature']}},
            {'year': {'$gte': 2023}},
        ]
    },
)
A common production pattern is to store all tenants' vectors in a single collection, isolated by a
metadata field, and enforce isolation through filters at query time.  This avoids the operational overhead of managing one collection per tenant.

# Shared collection with tenant isolation via filter

# Add documents with tenant ID
collection.add(
    ids=['t1-doc-1', 't1-doc-2'],
    documents=['Tenant 1 document A', 'Tenant 1 document B'],
    metadatas=[{'tenant_id': 'tenant-1'}, {'tenant_id': 'tenant-1'}],
)
collection.add(
    ids=['t2-doc-1'],
    documents=['Tenant 2 document A'],
    metadatas=[{'tenant_id': 'tenant-2'}],
)

# Query with mandatory tenant filter -- enforced in application layer
def query_for_tenant(query_text, tenant_id, n=5):
    return collection.query(
        query_texts=[query_text],
        n_results=n,
        where={'tenant_id': {'$eq': tenant_id}},
    )

results = query_for_tenant('machine learning', 'tenant-1')
# Tenant 2's documents never appear

# Pinecone: use namespaces for stronger isolation
# (namespace-level isolation, no cross-namespace leakage)
index.upsert(vectors=[...], namespace='tenant-1')
results = index.query(vector=emb, top_k=5, namespace='tenant-1')

Metadata filter isolation depends on the application always attaching the correct tenant_id filter to every query.  A bug that omits the filter exposes all tenants' data.  Pinecone namespaces and Weaviate/Milvus multi-tenancy features provide stronger isolation at the database level, where a missing filter cannot cross a namespace boundary.  For GDPR-regulated multi-tenant workloads, prefer namespace-level isolation.
Combining Metadata Filters with Hybrid Search
Hybrid search (dense vector similarity + sparse BM25 keyword matching) and metadata filtering compose orthogonally: you can filter the candidate pool by metadata

simultaneously ranking by both semantic similarity and keyword overlap.  This is particularly useful for RAG systems where users provide both a semantic query and structured constraints (e.g. "recent papers on HNSW" implies both a keyword match on HNSW and a date filter).

import weaviate, weaviate.classes as wvc

client = weaviate.connect_to_local()
articles = client.collections.get('Article')

# Hybrid search + metadata filter
# alpha=0.75: 75% dense vector, 25% BM25 keyword
results = articles.query.hybrid(
    query='HNSW approximate nearest neighbour graph',
    alpha=0.75,
    limit=5,
    filters=(
        wvc.query.Filter.by_property('source').equal('arxiv')
        & wvc.query.Filter.by_property('year').greater_than(2022)
    ),
    return_metadata=wvc.query.MetadataQuery(score=True),
)

for obj in results.objects:
    print(
        obj.properties.get('title'),
        f"score={obj.metadata.score:.3f}",
        f"source={obj.properties.get('source')}",
    )

# Milvus: sparse+dense hybrid with filter
# Both ANN and BM25 search respect the same filter expression
dense_results = client.search('articles', data=[dense_emb], limit=20,
                              filter='source == "arxiv" and year > 2022',
                              search_params={'metric_type': 'COSINE'})
sparse_results = client.search('articles', data=[sparse_vec], limit=20,
                               anns_field='sparse_embedding',
                               filter='source == "arxiv" and year > 2022',
                               search_params={'metric_type': 'IP'})
# RRF merge results (Milvus HybridSearch does this automatically)

client.close()
Three symptoms indicate a filtering problem in production:
Query returns 0 results despite matching documents existing.
Usually a post-filter with very selective predicate.  Check filter selectivity and switch to filter-aware ANN or increase n_results/over-fetch.
Queries with filters are 10-100x slower than unfiltered queries.
Usually missing scalar index in Milvus.  Check index existence on the filtered field and run a CREATE INDEX if absent.
Filter results differ from a brute-force check.
Metadata value mismatch (case sensitivity, type mismatch: string '2023' vs integer 2023).  Print the raw metadata of a few matching documents to verify the stored value format.

# Diagnostic: measure filter selectivity before choosing strategy
import chromadb

client = chromadb.PersistentClient(path='./chroma_db')
collection = client.get_collection('articles')

total = collection.count()
filtered = len(collection.get(
    where={'source': {'$eq': 'arxiv'}},
    include=[],  # don't fetch embeddings -- just count
)['ids'])

selectivity = filtered / total if total > 0 else 0
print(f'Total: {total}, Matching: {filtered}, Selectivity: {selectivity:.1%}')

if selectivity < 0.05:
    print('WARNING: Low selectivity -- post-filter may return 0 results')
    print('Recommendation: use filter-aware ANN (Weaviate/Milvus) or over-fetch by',
          round(1 / selectivity), 'x')
Using post-filter without over-fetching for selective predicates.

If source == 'rare-journal' matches only 0.5% of vectors, a post-filter with n_results=5 will almost always return 0 results.  Either switch to filter-aware ANN or over-fetch by at least 1/selectivity.
Not creating scalar indexes for frequently filtered fields in Milvus.
Without a TRIE or BITMAP index, Milvus must scan all segments to evaluate the filter, making pre-filter slower than post-filter.
Relying on metadata-only multi-tenancy for sensitive data.
A missing filter parameter in application code exposes all tenants' data.  Use Weaviate multi-tenancy, Milvus partitions, or Pinecone namespaces for strong isolation.
Storing high-cardinality values in a BITMAP index (Milvus).
BITMAP indexes work well for low-cardinality fields (< 500 distinct values).  For fields like user_id (millions of values), use TRIE or a hash-based index.
Confusing filter selectivity with filter cost.

A filter that selects 1% of records (very selective) is cheap to evaluate (TRIE lookup) but expensive for post-filter ANN (huge over-fetch needed).  A filter that selects 80% of records is expensive to evaluate (large bitset) but easy for post-filter (high hit rate).
Measure filter selectivity for each filter you plan to use.  A simple count query (collection.count() with the filter vs without) reveals the ratio and guides your strategy choice.
For filters with selectivity < 5%, use filter-aware ANN (Weaviate, Milvus) or pre-filter with a bitmap index.  Post-filter with over-fetch is not viable at this selectivity.

In Milvus, create scalar indexes (TRIE for strings, BITMAP for categoricals, STL_SORT for numerics) on every field used in filter expressions.  Without scalar indexes, the filter falls back to a full segment scan.

Always include the tenant_id filter in query-building code at the earliest possible point (ideally at the repository layer, not the controller).  Make it impossible to issue a query without the tenant constraint.

For RAG pipelines where you filter by source, tag, or date: store these values in metadata at insert time using consistent, normalized forms (e.g. ISO dates, lowercase source names).  Filter mismatches from inconsistent casing are a common silent failure.
Monitor filter result counts in production.  A filter that returns 0 results indicates either data is missing, the filter is too selective for post-filter mode, or a metadata value mismatch.
Metadata filtering combines ANN similarity search with scalar predicates.  The three strategies -- post-filter, pre-filter, and filter-aware ANN -- each suit a different range of filter selectivity.

Post-filter (ANN first, apply filter to results) is simple and fast for filters that match > 20% of the corpus.  Below 10% selectivity, it requires expensive over-fetching (K = n / selectivity) and fails below 1%.
Pre-filter (evaluate filter first, build candidate bitset, ANN within the set) gives correct recall at any selectivity but requires scalar indexes on filter fields.
Filter-aware ANN (HNSW traversal with per-node filter check) is the most flexible strategy -- Weaviate and Milvus both implement it.  Increase efSearch proportionally with selectivity.

For multi-tenant RAG, prefer database-level isolation (namespaces, partitions, Weaviate multi-tenancy) over metadata-only filter isolation to prevent accidental data leakage from a missing filter parameter.
Create scalar indexes (TRIE/BITMAP/STL_SORT in Milvus) on every field used in filter expressions to enable fast pre-filter evaluation.
